test_cases.label: HTTP client replacement across 200 call sites
test_cases.prompt: We need to replace our HTTP client library across 200 call sites in a live production service. We can't take a feature freeze to do it. What's the approach?
test_cases.expected_skill_use: Branch By Abstraction: (1) introduce a thin HTTPClient interface that wraps the current library; (2) migrate all 200 call sites to use the interface incrementally; (3) implement the new library behind the interface; (4) remove the old library. The code is never broken. Migration can span weeks distributed across normal work.

test_cases.label: Logging framework used everywhere
test_cases.prompt: We're using a deprecated logging library that's imported in 80 packages. We want to switch to slog but can't do it in one PR. How do we manage this migration?
test_cases.expected_skill_use: Branch By Abstraction: define a Logger interface matching how callers use the current library; implement it with the current library; gradually migrate packages to inject/use the interface; then swap the implementation to slog; remove the old library.

test_cases.label: Framework migration without a freeze
test_cases.prompt: We want to migrate from Gin to a newer web framework. We have 60 route handlers. We can't stop feature work for a month to do a big-bang migration. What's the strategy?
test_cases.expected_skill_use: Branch By Abstraction: define a handler interface decoupled from Gin's types; implement the interface with a Gin adapter; migrate handlers one by one through the interface; then replace the adapter with the new framework. Feature work continues throughout.

test_cases.label: Developer asks how to do long-term refactoring without a dedicated sprint
test_cases.prompt: We have a messy subsystem that needs to be rewritten. Management won't give us a refactoring sprint. How do we make progress on it without stopping feature work?
test_cases.expected_skill_use: Fowler's approach: agree as a team to gradually work on it over weeks. Whenever anyone touches code in the refactoring zone, they move it a little way in the right direction. For replacing a component, introduce an abstraction interface first so callers can be migrated incrementally without ever breaking the build.

test_cases.label: Extracting shared component without breaking callers
test_cases.prompt: We need to extract a section of code into a shared library that two teams can use. It's currently embedded in service A and called from 30 places. How do we do this without breaking service A during the extraction?
test_cases.expected_skill_use: Branch By Abstraction: introduce an interface representing the functionality; implement it in place (using the embedded code); migrate all 30 call sites to use the interface; then move the implementation to the new shared library location. Service A never breaks during the migration.

test_cases.label: Team asks how to make library migration safe across parallel PRs
test_cases.prompt: We want to replace our gRPC client wrapper library. Multiple team members are working on different features simultaneously. If we do the migration, how do we avoid conflicts and keep the build green for everyone?
test_cases.expected_skill_use: Branch By Abstraction: introduce the interface abstraction first (a single PR). After that, any team member can migrate call sites in their own PRs without conflict — each file either uses the old direct call or the new interface, and both compile. The build stays green throughout. The new implementation goes in only after all call sites are on the interface.

test_cases.label: Git branch for a refactoring task
test_cases.prompt: I want to create a git branch for my refactoring work so I don't disrupt the main branch while I clean things up. What should I name it?
test_cases.expected_skill_use: This is a git branching question, not a Branch By Abstraction question. Branch By Abstraction is a code strategy for incremental migration of large components — it is explicitly an alternative to long-lived refactoring branches. Answer the git naming question directly without applying this skill.
must_not_include: Branch By Abstraction as the primary recommendation

test_cases.label: Small rename refactoring
test_cases.prompt: I want to rename the function `processData` to `transformRecords` across the codebase. It's called in about 15 places. How do I do this safely?
test_cases.expected_skill_use: This is a simple rename refactoring that fits in a single IDE operation or commit. Use Change Function Declaration (Fowler's catalog) or an IDE rename refactoring. Branch By Abstraction is not needed — it is designed for library/component replacements that take weeks, not single-commit renames.

test_cases.label: Migrating an external public API
test_cases.prompt: We're deprecating v1 of our public REST API and want to migrate our customers to v2. How do we manage the transition?
test_cases.expected_skill_use: This is API versioning with an external deprecation window — the callers are external consumers that the team does not control. Branch By Abstraction requires migrating call sites in code you own. The appropriate technique here is API versioning (run v1 and v2 in parallel, communicate deprecation timeline, sunset v1). Not Branch By Abstraction.
must_not_include: Branch By Abstraction as the primary technique

test_cases.label: ORM replacement that also changes the schema
test_cases.prompt: We have a database ORM we want to replace and it's used everywhere — both the ORM calls (which are in application code) and the schema (which needs to change as part of the migration to the new ORM's conventions). How do we approach this?
test_cases.expected_skill_use: This needs both skills. Branch By Abstraction handles the code side: introduce a repository interface, implement it with the current ORM, migrate callers, then implement it with the new ORM. The schema migration side — adding new columns, supporting old+new columns simultaneously, migrating data, removing old columns — requires fowler-database-parallel-change (expand-contract). These two techniques are complementary and both apply here.

test_cases.label: Replacing a component that crosses a service boundary
test_cases.prompt: We want to replace our internal payment processing service with a third-party provider. Our application makes gRPC calls to the internal service from about 40 places. How do we migrate?
test_cases.expected_skill_use: Branch By Abstraction applies to the code side: define a PaymentProcessor interface; implement it with the current gRPC client; migrate all 40 call sites to the interface; then implement the interface with the third-party SDK. However, this crosses a network boundary — the implementation swap involves production traffic to a live external service, not just an in-process library swap. Additional concerns apply: traffic shadowing, rollback strategy, monitoring the new provider before full cutover. Branch By Abstraction handles the code migration; network-level migration safety requires additional patterns.

test_cases.label: Partial migration already underway
test_cases.prompt: We started migrating from our old HTTP client to a new one six months ago. We're about 60% through the call sites. The migration has stalled and now we have a mix of old and new code in the codebase. What should we do?
test_cases.expected_skill_use: This is a stalled Branch By Abstraction migration. The partially-migrated state is a known failure mode of the technique. Options: (a) recommit — assign ownership, use CI to track remaining direct usages of the old library, complete the migration; (b) if the migration cannot be completed, standardize on one or the other rather than living permanently in the in-between state. The worst outcome is leaving the migration permanently partial — callers split across two paths make both libraries hard to reason about and impossible to safely remove.
